edges.description: Loss of VPS13A disrupts its normal bridge-like lipid-transfer role at membrane contact sites.
edges.source: VPS13A Loss of Function and Chorein Deficiency
edges.target: Membrane Contact Site Dysfunction with Impaired Lipid Transport

edges.description: VPS13A loss perturbs red-cell lipid handling and chorein-sensitive actin membrane cytoskeleton organization, creating the hematologic branch of the disease pathograph.
intermediate_mechanisms: altered erythrocyte lipid species and chorein-sensitive beta-adducin/actin organization
edges.source: VPS13A Loss of Function and Chorein Deficiency
edges.target: Erythrocyte Lipid and Actin Cytoskeleton Remodeling

edges.description: VPS13A deficiency produces actin- and Src/Lyn kinase-associated synaptic dysregulation in patient-derived striatal medium spiny neurons.
intermediate_mechanisms: elevated Lyn/Src kinase activity and increased G/F-actin ratio
edges.source: VPS13A Loss of Function and Chorein Deficiency
edges.target: Striatal Medium Spiny Neuron Synaptic Dysfunction

edges.description: Absence of VPS13A impairs autophagy and produces downstream muscle metabolic injury.
edges.source: VPS13A Loss of Function and Chorein Deficiency

edges.description: Loss of a bridge-like lipid-transfer protein at membrane contact sites leads to altered patient erythrocyte lipid species, a plausible upstream driver of red-cell shape change.
intermediate_mechanisms: altered phosphatidylethanolamine, ceramide, phosphatidylcholine, and sphingomyelin species
edges.source: Membrane Contact Site Dysfunction with Impaired Lipid Transport
edges.target: Erythrocyte Lipid and Actin Cytoskeleton Remodeling

edges.description: Abnormal erythrocyte lipid composition and membrane cytoskeletal mechanics produce thorn-like red-cell deformation.
edges.source: Erythrocyte Lipid and Actin Cytoskeleton Remodeling

edges.description: Medium spiny neurons are the primary striatal neuronal population affected in the disease, placing synaptic dysfunction upstream of basal ganglia degeneration in the pathograph.
intermediate_mechanisms: actin-dependent synaptic dysfunction and striatal neuronal vulnerability
edges.source: Striatal Medium Spiny Neuron Synaptic Dysfunction

edges.description: Pathologically elevated neuronal synaptic activity provides a plausible neuronal route to seizures, although the circuit-level intermediates are not fully resolved.
edges.source: Striatal Medium Spiny Neuron Synaptic Dysfunction

edges.description: VPS13A-related muscle and nerve involvement produces hyperCKemia as a measurable laboratory readout of the neuromuscular branch.
Progressive neuromuscular involvement can manifest clinically as weakness and wasting.
VPS13A-associated neuromuscular involvement frequently includes reduced reflexes.
Peripheral nerve involvement in VPS13A disease can produce sensorimotor axonal neuropathy.
Striatal and caudate degeneration disrupts basal ganglia motor circuits, producing choreiform hyperkinesia.
Basal ganglia circuit dysfunction contributes to the prominent orofacial dyskinesia and dystonia characteristic of ChAc.
intermediate_mechanisms: disrupted basal ganglia motor output to orobulbar motor control circuits

edges.description: Basal ganglia motor-circuit dysfunction manifests as dystonia, including feeding and orolingual dystonia.
intermediate_mechanisms: disrupted basal ganglia motor output to cranial and limb motor circuits

edges.description: Basal ganglia circuit dysfunction can present with tic-like vocal phenomena in genetically confirmed ChAc.
intermediate_mechanisms: disrupted basal ganglia motor output and tic-generation circuits

edges.description: Orolingual and feeding dystonia from basal-ganglia motor circuit dysfunction impairs swallowing.
Basal ganglia and striatal degeneration is part of the broader frontostriatal neurodegenerative phenotype that includes cognitive decline.
Frontostriatal neurodegeneration contributes to the behavioral and psychiatric disease manifestations reported in ChAc.

nodes.description: Spiculated erythrocytes are characteristic but may be absent early or on individual smears; their absence does not exclude the diagnosis.

evidence.reference_title: Two case reports of chorea-acanthocytosis and review of literature.
evidence.snippet: Chorea-acanthocytosis (ChAc), as the most common subtype of neuroacanthocytosis syndrome, is characterized by the presence of acanthocytes and neurological symptoms.

evidence.reference_title: [Chorea-acanthocytosis without acanthocytes].
evidence.snippet: Absence or late appearance of acanthocytes in ChAc has been described in a few case reports.

nodes.description: Neurodegeneration preferentially affects the basal ganglia, especially the caudate and striatum. This structural involvement aligns with the hyperkinetic movement disorder and bulbar orolingual manifestations typical of chorea-acanthocytosis.

evidence.reference_title: Clinico-genetic profile of case series of six Tamilian chorea-acanthocytosis families with VPS13A mutations from South India.
evidence.snippet: Chorea-acanthocytosis (ChAc) is a rare genetic disorder characterized by acanthocytosis and basal ganglia degeneration.

evidence.reference_title: Clinico-genetic profile of case series of six Tamilian chorea-acanthocytosis families with VPS13A mutations from South India.
evidence.snippet: Brain MRI showed caudate atrophy, and acanthocytes were detected in three patients.

nodes.description: Choreiform hyperkinesia is one of the central motor manifestations and commonly drives initial comparison with Huntington disease.

evidence.reference_title: A chorea-acanthocytosis patient with novel mutations in the VPS13A gene without acanthocyte.
evidence.snippet: Chorea-acanthocytosis (ChAc) is a rare clinical genetic disorder of the nervous system, which is characterized by choreiform movement disorder, cognitive decline, and psychiatric disorders.

nodes.description: Progressive cognitive decline is part of the neuropsychiatric phenotype and can accompany the movement disorder.

evidence.reference_title: A chorea-acanthocytosis patient with novel mutations in the VPS13A gene without acanthocyte.
evidence.snippet: Chorea-acanthocytosis (ChAc) is a rare clinical genetic disorder of the nervous system, which is characterized by choreiform movement disorder, cognitive decline, and psychiatric disorders.

nodes.description: Swallowing impairment is common, often occurring together with orolingual dystonia or dyskinesia and feeding dystonia.

evidence.reference_title: Clinico-genetic profile of case series of six Tamilian chorea-acanthocytosis families with VPS13A mutations from South India.
evidence.snippet: All presented with feeding dystonia, vocal tics, and dysphagia.

nodes.description: Dystonia, particularly feeding or orolingual dystonia, is a characteristic motor manifestation that contributes to swallowing and feeding problems.

evidence.reference_title: Clinico-genetic profile of case series of six Tamilian chorea-acanthocytosis families with VPS13A mutations from South India.
evidence.snippet: All presented with feeding dystonia, vocal tics, and dysphagia.

nodes.description: HyperCKemia is a common early laboratory clue that reflects associated muscle involvement, even when overt weakness is limited.

evidence.reference_title: [Chorea-acanthocytosis without acanthocytes].
evidence.snippet: Routine biological screening was normal except for elevated CPK and LDH.

evidence.reference_title: The Diverse Neuromuscular Spectrum of VPS13A Disease.
evidence.snippet: Phosphocreatine kinase was elevated in all cases

nodes.description: Patient erythrocytes show altered lipid species, abnormal blood-cell mechanics, and chorein-sensitive membrane cytoskeletal changes. This red-cell branch explains how VPS13A deficiency produces acanthocyte morphology independently of the neuronal phenotype.
nodes.id: Erythrocyte Lipid and Actin Cytoskeleton Remodeling

evidence.reference_title: Phosphatidylethanolamines are the Main Lipid Class Altered in Red Blood Cells from Patients with VPS13A Disease/Chorea-Acanthocytosis.
evidence.snippet: alterations in certain species were detected: phosphatidylethanolamine species with both longer chain length and higher unsaturation were increased in VPS13A disease samples.

evidence.reference_title: Changes in Blood Cell Deformability in Chorea-Acanthocytosis and Effects of Treatment With Dasatinib or Lithium.
evidence.snippet: The hematological phenotype of ChAc patients hinted at a reorganization of the cytoskeleton in blood cells which partly explains the altered mechanical properties observed here.

evidence.reference_title: Chorein, the protein responsible for chorea-acanthocytosis, interacts with β-adducin and β-actin.
evidence.snippet: Protein levels of β-adducin isoform 1 and β-actin are markedly decreased in erythrocyte membranes from a ChAc patient.

nodes.description: Reduced reflexes are a frequent neuromuscular sign in genetically confirmed VPS13A disease.

evidence.reference_title: The Diverse Neuromuscular Spectrum of VPS13A Disease.
evidence.snippet: Neuromuscular signs ranged from hyporeflexia (5/6) to progressive muscle wasting (3/6).

nodes.description: VPS13A deficiency impairs autophagy and produces metabolic remodeling with oxidative damage and premature muscle aging. This mechanism plausibly contributes to the hyperCKemia and subclinical myopathy often seen early in the disease course.

evidence.reference_title: Premature skeletal muscle aging in VPS13A deficiency relates to impaired autophagy.
evidence.snippet: The absence of Vps13A impaired autophagy, resulting in pathologic metabolic remodeling characterized by cellular energy depletion, increased protein/lipid oxidation and a hyperactivated unfolded protein response.

evidence.reference_title: Premature skeletal muscle aging in VPS13A deficiency relates to impaired autophagy.
evidence.snippet: The biological relevance of our mouse findings, supported by human muscle biopsy data, shed new light on the role of VPS13A in muscle homeostasis.

evidence.reference_title: The Diverse Neuromuscular Spectrum of VPS13A Disease.
evidence.snippet: Neuromuscular signs ranged from hyporeflexia (5/6) to progressive muscle wasting (3/6).

nodes.description: VPS13A normally acts as a bridge-like lipid transfer protein at membrane contact sites. Loss of VPS13A disrupts intracellular lipid distribution and perturbs mitochondrial calcium handling, consistent with organelle homeostasis failure in patient-derived cells.
nodes.id: Membrane Contact Site Dysfunction with Impaired Lipid Transport

evidence.reference_title: VPS13A Deficiency Leads to Impaired Lipid Distribution and Alteration of Mitochondrial Calcium Homeostasis in Fibroblasts of VPS13A Disease Patients.
evidence.snippet: VPS13A is a membrane-residing, bridge-like protein connecting two membranes to enable bulk lipid transfer.

evidence.reference_title: VPS13A Deficiency Leads to Impaired Lipid Distribution and Alteration of Mitochondrial Calcium Homeostasis in Fibroblasts of VPS13A Disease Patients.
evidence.snippet: We observed a general disturbance of membrane contact sites in VPS13A disease, accompanied by a reduction in lipid droplet formation, diminished lipid transfer into mitochondria, and unusual mitochondrial calcium uptake behavior in VPS13A disease fibroblasts.

nodes.description: Weakness can occur within the underrecognized neuromuscular spectrum of VPS13A disease and may precede prominent hyperkinetic movement symptoms.

evidence.reference_title: The Diverse Neuromuscular Spectrum of VPS13A Disease.
evidence.snippet: Neuromuscular signs ranged from hyporeflexia (5/6) to progressive muscle wasting (3/6).

nodes.description: Off-label dasatinib has been evaluated in a small human ChAc series as a tyrosine kinase inhibitor strategy aimed at Lyn/Src-linked cytoskeletal and autophagy abnormalities in blood cells; clinical CNS benefit is unproven.

evidence.reference_title: Changes in Blood Cell Deformability in Chorea-Acanthocytosis and Effects of Treatment With Dasatinib or Lithium.
evidence.snippet: Here, we investigated blood cell deformability of five ChAc patients compared to healthy controls during up to 1-year individual off-label treatment with the tyrosine kinase inhibitor dasatinib or several weeks with lithium.

nodes.description: Prominent involuntary mouth, tongue, and facial movements are highly suggestive of chorea-acanthocytosis and often lead to tongue or lip biting.

evidence.reference_title: Chorea-acanthocytosis: a mimicker of Huntington disease case report and review of the literature.
evidence.snippet: Clinical clues suggestive of chorea-acanthocytosis include prominent orofacial dyskinesias, often causing dysarthria and dysphagia.

nodes.description: Sensorimotor axonal neuropathy expands the ChAc phenotype beyond central basal-ganglia neurodegeneration.

evidence.reference_title: The Diverse Neuromuscular Spectrum of VPS13A Disease.
evidence.snippet: Nerve conduction studies revealed sensorimotor axonal neuropathy.

nodes.description: Behavioral and psychiatric manifestations are common in ChAc and can accompany cognitive decline and the movement disorder.

evidence.reference_title: A chorea-acanthocytosis patient with novel mutations in the VPS13A gene without acanthocyte.
evidence.snippet: Chorea-acanthocytosis (ChAc) is a rare clinical genetic disorder of the nervous system, which is characterized by choreiform movement disorder, cognitive decline, and psychiatric disorders.

nodes.description: Epileptic seizures occur in a meaningful subset of patients and may be an early presenting feature before the full movement disorder is recognized.

evidence.reference_title: A chorea-acanthocytosis patient with novel mutations in the VPS13A gene without acanthocyte.
evidence.snippet: Here, we report a patient, who has the characteristic clinical manifestations of ChAc with limb choreiform movements, involuntary lip and tongue bites, seizures, and emotional instability.

nodes.description: ChAc patient-derived striatal medium spiny neurons show pathologically elevated synaptic activity linked to actin cytoskeleton and Src/Lyn kinase dysregulation. This neuronal dysfunction provides an intermediate between VPS13A loss and basal-ganglia-driven movement and seizure manifestations.
nodes.id: Striatal Medium Spiny Neuron Synaptic Dysfunction

evidence.reference_title: Neuronal Dysfunction in iPSC-Derived Medium Spiny Neurons from Chorea-Acanthocytosis Patients Is Reversed by Src Kinase Inhibition and F-Actin Stabilization.
evidence.snippet: Electrophysiological analysis revealed a pathologically elevated synaptic activity in ChAc MSNs.

evidence.reference_title: Neuronal Dysfunction in iPSC-Derived Medium Spiny Neurons from Chorea-Acanthocytosis Patients Is Reversed by Src Kinase Inhibition and F-Actin Stabilization.
evidence.snippet: These data indicate that F-actin stabilization and Src kinase inhibition represent potential therapeutic targets in ChAc that may restore neuronal function.

evidence.reference_title: [Chorea-acanthocytosis without acanthocytes].
evidence.snippet: ChAc is an autosomal recessive disorder due to mutations of the VPS13A gene coding for chorein.

evidence.reference_title: A chorea-acanthocytosis patient with novel mutations in the VPS13A gene without acanthocyte.
evidence.snippet: We later identified two novel pathogenic mutations in the patient's vacuolar protein sorting homolog 13 A (VPS13A) on chromosome 9q21 by targeted gene sequencing, and she was definitively diagnosed with "ChAc."

nodes.description: Chorea-acanthocytosis is caused by biallelic loss of VPS13A, which encodes chorein, a large membrane-contact-site protein. Loss of chorein is the initiating molecular defect for downstream lipid-transport, autophagy, and neurodegenerative abnormalities.
nodes.id: VPS13A Loss of Function and Chorein Deficiency

evidence.reference_title: [Chorea-acanthocytosis without acanthocytes].
evidence.snippet: ChAc is an autosomal recessive disorder due to mutations of the VPS13A gene coding for chorein.

evidence.reference_title: Two case reports of chorea-acanthocytosis and review of literature.
evidence.snippet: It is thought to be caused by the VPS13A (vacuolar protein sorting-associated protein 13A) mutations.

nodes.description: Vocal tic-like phenomena can occur as part of the basal-ganglia motor phenotype in ChAc.

evidence.reference_title: Clinico-genetic profile of case series of six Tamilian chorea-acanthocytosis families with VPS13A mutations from South India.
evidence.snippet: All presented with feeding dystonia, vocal tics, and dysphagia.
